item.title: Simulating Bouncing Balls with Collisions in Python using Pygame
item.author: Dmitry Romanoff
item.content:encoded:
In this article, we will walk through a Python program that simulates bouncing balls within a window, using the Pygame library. The code also includes functionality for detecting and resolving collisions between the balls. This example provides a good introduction to both basic physics in programming and how to use Pygame to create dynamic simulations.Setting up Pygame for creating a simulation.Implementing basic motion of balls with random velocity.Detecting collisions between balls and resolving them according to physical principles.Using object-oriented programming (OOP) in Python to structure the simulation.Setting Up the EnvironmentBefore diving into the code, we need to ensure that Pygame is installed. If you haven’t installed Pygame yet, you can do so using pip:Here’s an overview of how the program works:
1. Pygame Initialization and Display SetupThe code begins by initializing Pygame and setting up the display. The window dimensions are 800x600 pixels, and each ball in the simulation has a radius of 20 pixels.These are RGB color definitions used to render balls and the background. In this case, we have three colors for the balls (red, green, blue) and two colors for the background (white and black).
3. Ball Class: Managing Individual BallsThe class is central to this program. Each ball object has properties like position (, ), velocity (, ), and color. The ball's initial position and velocity are set randomly, so each ball starts in a different location and moves at a unique speed.
4. Moving the Balls and Handling Boundary BouncesThe method updates the ball's position based on its velocity. The program handles wall collisions by reversing the velocity along the respective axis when a wall is hit, making the ball bounce off the walls.
5. Drawing the Balls on the ScreenThe method uses Pygame’s function to render each ball on the screen with its corresponding color and position.
6. Handling Ball-to-Ball CollisionsThe method handles the detection of collisions between two balls. It calculates the distance between the centers of two balls. If the distance is less than the sum of their radii (i.e., they overlap), the program applies basic physics principles to resolve the collision by adjusting the balls' velocities and positions. This ensures that the balls bounce off each other in a realistic manner.The main game loop continuously updates the positions of the balls, checks for collisions, and renders the screen. The window is filled with a white background at the start of each loop iteration, and then each ball is drawn on top. The loop runs at 60 frames per second ().When the user closes the window, Pygame shuts down and the program terminates.Here is the full code for the simulation:This simple simulation demonstrates the basics of object-oriented programming in Python, along with an introduction to collision detection and physics simulations. With Pygame, you can create dynamic and interactive visualizations, making it an excellent choice for creating games and simulations.

item.title: Python vs JavaScript: Which Wins for Web Dev?
item.author: Resource Bunk
item.content:encoded:
🎉 BOTH OF THEM HAVING SPECIAL DISCOUNT FROM HERE, CHECK IT NOW.“When speed meets simplicity, the choices you make today shape the technology of tomorrow.”In today’s fast-evolving web development landscape, the choice between Python and JavaScript isn’t just about language syntax—it’s about picking a toolset that fits your project’s performance needs, scalability goals, and development style. In this ultimate showdown, we dive deep into full-stack development by pitting popular frameworks against each other: Flask vs. Node.js FastAPI vs. Deno Django vs. Express HTMX vs. ReactThis guide is designed to give you actionable insights, real code samples, performance stats, and plenty of resources to help you choose the best technology for your project.
1. Speed Test: Flask vs. Node.js is a lightweight Python micro-framework ideal for rapid prototyping and smaller applications. Its minimal design means you can get a basic web server running with just a few lines of code:While Flask is simple and clean, its synchronous, single-threaded nature can become a bottleneck under high concurrency., on the other hand, uses an asynchronous, event-driven model based on Chrome’s V8 engine. With frameworks like Express, you can quickly build a server that handles thousands of simultaneous connections:: Often handles tens of thousands of requests per second under I/O-bound scenarios.: Typically handles a few thousand RPS when using development servers like the built-in Werkzeug server. For production, you might use Gunicorn with Flask, which can boost performance but still tends to lag behind Node.js in high-concurrency scenarios. Consider using asynchronous libraries like Gevent or running your Flask app with Gunicorn and multiple workers to mitigate concurrency limitations.For a simple “Hello World” endpoint, a test using wrk might show:Flask (Gunicorn with 4 workers): ~1,200–1,500 RPS ~4,500–5,000 RPSThis data shows that for applications requiring extremely high throughput, Node.js may be more appropriate.
2. Async Showdown: FastAPI vs. Deno
FastAPI: Modern Python for Fast APIs FastAPI leverages Python’s async/await syntax to build APIs that are both high-performing and easy to maintain. Here’s a sample FastAPI app: In benchmarks, FastAPI has been shown to handle:Low-latency I/O-bound operations: Up to 4,000–5,000 RPS on a single worker (and even higher when scaled with multiple workers). Its automatic generation of interactive API documentation (Swagger and ReDoc) saves valuable development time and reduces bugs.
Deno: A Secure Modern Alternative Deno is a secure JavaScript/TypeScript runtime designed to fix many of Node.js’ shortcomings. Its async nature and built-in security make it a competitive choice for building APIs.Here’s a simple Deno HTTP server example using the Oak framework:Deno’s asynchronous model ensures high throughput and low latency, often rivaling Node.js in many benchmarks.Both Deno and FastAPI excel in asynchronous environments, but your choice might depend on your team's language preference.
3. Backend Ease-of-Use: Django vs. Express
Django: The “Batteries-Included” Python Framework Django is a robust, full-featured framework with built-in tools for ORM, authentication, and an admin interface. Here’s a simple Django view:Rich ecosystem with extensive documentation.Admin panel and built-in user management.Ideal for large-scale applications requiring robust data handling. Large-scale Django deployments (e.g., Instagram) demonstrate that even with a heavy feature set, Django can efficiently scale when properly optimized.
Express: Minimal and Flexible JavaScript Express provides a minimalist framework for Node.js, perfect for those who need full flexibility. Here’s a typical Express route:Minimalistic yet powerful for building RESTful APIs.A huge ecosystem via npm.Highly flexible architecture that pairs well with real-time data."Express's lightweight nature and modular design make it a go-to solution for many full-stack JavaScript applications."Tech Insights, HackerNoon For teams already immersed in JavaScript or building real-time applications, Express is an excellent choice. Conversely, if you require a rich set of features out-of-the-box and prefer Python, Django might be the way to go.
4. Front-End Integration: HTMX vs. React
HTMX: Minimal JavaScript, Maximum Simplicity HTMX lets you add interactivity using HTML attributes without heavy JavaScript frameworks. It’s perfect for applications where you want to keep things simple.HTMX Demo Get Weather No need to write a lot of JavaScript.Great integration with Python backends like Flask or Django.Ideal for simple, dynamic pages without the overhead of a full SPA.
React: Dynamic, Component-Based UI React offers a robust solution for building interactive user interfaces with a rich ecosystem.Ideal for complex front-end applications.A vast community and extensive libraries.State management and component reusability boost productivity."React’s component-based architecture and powerful ecosystem make it a top choice for building scalable and interactive UIs."Frontend Insights, Medium For smaller projects or when you want to add quick interactivity, HTMX is a brilliant option. If your project demands a highly interactive, stateful UI, React remains one of the best tools available.
Key Considerations for Choosing Your Tech Stack High Concurrency & Low Latency: If you expect your application to handle thousands of concurrent users or need ultra-low latency, (or modern alternatives like ) is a clear winner.Rapid Prototyping & Developer Productivity: For quick MVPs, data-driven applications, or when integrating with Python-based data science tools, and offer simplicity and speed of development.Full-Featured Applications: If you need a “batteries-included” solution with robust built-in features (like an admin panel, ORM, and security features), is hard to beat.Unified Frontend & Backend: For teams that prefer to maintain a consistent JavaScript codebase, using with and a front-end framework like or even can streamline development.Minimalist, Interactive Front Ends: When you need a lightweight solution to add interactivity without a heavy JavaScript framework, can be a refreshing alternative.
Resources and Further Reading "Benchmark your applications under real-world conditions using tools like wrk or Apache Bench. Understanding your app’s bottlenecks is key to optimization."Choosing between Python and JavaScript—and by extension, between their frameworks—comes down to the unique needs of your project. Consider:What tasks will your application perform?How many users are you expecting?Do you prioritize rapid development or maximum performance?Is it more important to have a unified codebase or to leverage specialized tools in each area?By exploring these questions, you can build a tech stack that aligns with your goals. Remember, there’s no one-size-fits-all solution. Experiment with small projects, measure performance, and most importantly, choose a stack that keeps you and your team both productive and motivated.Now is the time to dive in with confidence. With tools like Flask, FastAPI, Express, and modern runtimes such as Deno, you have all the power you need at your fingertips. And if you’re looking for more inspiration and resources along the way, don’t forget to visit —your one-stop hub for all things Python."Embrace your chosen tools, stay curious, and build something extraordinary. Your next groundbreaking application is just one decision away."Go ahead—experiment, learn, and let your project soar to new heights!
🚀 Want More Traffic and Income? Here’s Your Shortcut! If you’re serious about growing your website and driving real traffic, you can’t afford to ignore . It’s a hidden goldmine for niche websites, and the best part? You don’t need ads, a huge following, or a big budget.I’ve put together the Complete Pinterest Profit System Bundle to help you turn simple pins into website visitors, and website visitors into real income.✅ – No fluff, just actionable steps. – So you never miss a key step.Pinterest “Profit Cookbook” – Easy-to-follow strategies that work. – Small, consistent moves that bring big results. No gimmicks. No vague theories. Just real strategies that work. If you’ve ever wondered how to get steady traffic without SEO struggles, this is your answer.

item.title: Python DDoS Scripts: Dead or Still Dangerous?
item.author: Snappy Tuts
item.content:encoded:
In today's ever-evolving cybersecurity landscape, Python-based DDoS scripts may seem like a relic of the past—but they’re far from dead. While modern anti-DDoS defenses have grown smarter, attackers are constantly evolving, and Python remains a potent tool in the hands of cybercriminals. In this article, we break down the current state of Python DDoS scripts, explore the evolution of botnet frameworks, reveal key statistics and defenses, and offer actionable tips to fortify your defenses.“Cybersecurity isn’t a static field; both attackers and defenders continuously learn and adapt.”
1. The Changing Face of Python DDoS Scripts
How Python Became a Popular Weapon Python’s simplicity, vast library ecosystem, and rapid prototyping capabilities made it a go-to language for attackers looking to create denial-of-service (DoS) and distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) scripts. Even though many of these scripts are now well-known, their modularity allows for quick modifications and adaptations.
Code Example: A Simple Python DDoS Script Below is a simplified snippet (for educational purposes only) demonstrating how a Python script might send a flood of requests to a target server:This example is strictly for educational purposes to illustrate how Python can be misused if proper safeguards are not implemented.
Evolving Attack Frameworks Over time, Python-based tools have evolved from basic flood scripts to sophisticated frameworks that control thousands of compromised machines (botnets). Modern frameworks now support: Easy integration of new attack vectors. Attack coordination across distributed systems. Mimicking legitimate traffic to bypass simple detection.
2. How Modern Anti-DDoS Solutions Counter Python Attacks
Traffic Analysis and Behavioral Detection Modern defenses analyze traffic in real time. Systems now learn what “normal” traffic looks like, enabling them to flag anomalies, even if they originate from Python scripts. For example, if a Python script sends thousands of repetitive HTTP requests, intelligent systems using machine learning can quickly identify this deviation.
Signature and Behavioral Matching Anti-DDoS solutions use a combination of signature matching and behavioral analysis: Known patterns of Python scripts (such as specific library calls) are catalogued. By measuring metrics like request rates, header consistency, and error rates, these systems can differentiate between legitimate traffic and automated Python-based attacks.
Code Integration Example: Alerting on Abnormal Traffic Below is a Python snippet that could be part of a defensive system to log unusual traffic patterns:In production, this would integrate with a larger SIEM system to automate alerts and responses.Companies like Cloudflare now employ adaptive AI to not only detect but also predict DDoS attacks. These systems learn traffic patterns over days and trigger defenses when anomalies exceed preset thresholds.
3. Practical Defense Strategies for Python DDoS Scripts
Network Hygiene and Patch Management Keeping systems up to date is crucial: Patch servers and IoT devices to remove known vulnerabilities. Isolate critical infrastructure to reduce lateral movement during an attack.
Rate Limiting and WAF Rules Implement robust rate-limiting rules:Web Application Firewall (WAF): Configure your WAF to block traffic with abnormal patterns.Adaptive DDoS Protection: Use services that automatically adjust thresholds based on traffic behavior.Cloudflare’s Adaptive DDoS Protection uses a 95th-percentile error rate method to detect anomalies and automatically mitigate threats.
Community Engagement and Ongoing Education Stay updated with the latest trends and share knowledge. For developers looking to delve deeper into Python and cybersecurity, check out these resources:Leveraging community-driven platforms can keep you on the cutting edge of cybersecurity trends and Python development best practices.
4. Detailed Statistics and Recent Trends
Global DDoS Attack Trends According to recent reports:Over 21.3 million DDoS attacks were mitigated in 2024. (exceeding 1 Tbps) increased dramatically, with some attacks peaking at .Cloudflare’s network capacity now exceeds , reflecting a significant ramp-up in infrastructure to counter these threats.
Attack Vectors and Techniques TCP SYN floods, UDP floods, and ICMP floods—each with variable rates (high-rate vs. low-rate) to evade detection.Botnets powered by IoT devices: For instance, some botnets harness the power of millions of compromised smart devices, making it difficult to trace attack origins.Modern defense systems can detect and mitigate attacks in , thanks to real-time machine learning models and globally distributed networks."Fast response is critical—every second counts in preventing service disruptions during a DDoS attack."
5. Building a Resilient Defense with Python Combining Python with other technologies can lead to a comprehensive DDoS defense system: Write scripts that monitor traffic patterns. Automate defensive responses such as blocking IPs or scaling resources.ELK Stack for Monitoring: Use Elasticsearch, Logstash, and Kibana to visualize traffic trends and pinpoint anomalies.ElastAlert for Notifications: Set up rules to trigger alerts when suspicious activities occur.
Integrated Workflow Diagram Imagine an end-to-end system where: continuously analyze network logs. automate countermeasures upon detecting anomalies. provides real-time dashboards. sends immediate notifications to your security team.
Hands-On Workshop and Resources For developers eager to build or enhance their own DDoS detection systems, consider these resources:"Always test your defenses in a controlled environment before deploying them in production."
6. Final Thoughts and Actionable Insights Python DDoS scripts may not be as effective as they once were, thanks to adaptive and intelligent defenses. However, the threat remains real—attackers are continuously refining their methods. To stay ahead:Invest in robust DDoS protection solutions that leverage AI and machine learning.Continuously educate your team on emerging trends and techniques. like python.0x3d.site to stay updated and share best practices."The key to security is continuous improvement—adapt, learn, and fortify your defenses every day."Remember, every network is unique. Analyze your traffic, set up alerts, and test your response plans. With the right blend of technology, community insights, and proactive defense, you can mitigate the risks posed by even the most sophisticated Python-based DDoS attacks.Take these insights, apply them to your context, and let your defenses evolve as quickly as the threats. Your vigilance is your best asset in the battle for a secure digital future.By following this guide and leveraging the actionable tips provided, you’ll be better equipped to understand, detect, and defend against DDoS attacks—whether they’re powered by Python scripts or more complex botnets. Stay informed, stay protected, and remember that continuous learning is the cornerstone of effective cybersecurity.For more in-depth discussions, code samples, and cutting-edge resources, bookmark —your curated hub for Python developer resources.Disclaimer: The code examples and resources provided here are for educational purposes only. Always use ethical practices and ensure you have authorization before testing any security measures on a network.

item.title: argument passing in python
item.author: IIS ANISA
item.content:encoded:
in python when you execute your python files using interactive mode in python you could also pass the argument by adding it alongside with the filenames that you want to execute for examplepython tes.py 1 2 3 yes no you can get the list of argument above in the code by using lib built in form python called sys.you can call all of the argument that you pass when you execute the files by using thisimport sys
print(sys.argv) by excuting above code you will get all of the argument include the file name into the list.['tes.py',1,2,3,'yes','no'] or you could access name file only by using code bellow, because by default files name always placed in the first list.print(sys.argv[0]) #it will return-->tes.py

item.title: I Compared OpenAI Agents SDK, LangGraph, AutoGen, and CrewAI—Here’s What I Found!
item.author: Developer Harsh
item.content:encoded:
AI agents are no longer just a buzzword—they're the hottest trend of 2025! Everyone on Twitter, Reddit, and LinkedIn is talking about them, and new tools seem to emerge every week. With so many options, it’s easy to feel overwhelmed.The recent launch of has added another compelling choice to this ecosystem. But does it truly stand out?In this blog, I’ll break down the OpenAI Agents SDK and compare it with LangGraph, CrewAI, and AutoGen—helping you choose the best framework for your needs.OpenAI Agents SDK offers a lightweight, production-ready framework with minimal abstractions, making it accessible for developers new to agent development. I was able to build an agent in just 10 minutes of code tinkering.It has three core primitives: Agents, Handoffs, and Guardrails, providing a balance between simplicity and power.Agent SDK excels in tracing and visualization capabilities, making debugging and monitoring agent workflows straightforward. offers superior state management and graph-based workflows, ideal for complex, cyclical agent interactions and production, but have a steeper learning curveCrew AI provides the most intuitive approach to role-based multi-agent systems. Its only good for crew-based architecture and still worked on deployment and scalability.Auto Gen offers strong support for diverse conversation patterns and agent topologies. Learning curve is medium and docs are not so clear.Your choice should depend on your specific use case:
- simplicity and production, - complex workflows, intuitive multi-agent crew-based systems, flexible conversation patterns.
Deep Dive into OpenAI Agents SDK OpenAI's Agents SDK is a lightweight yet powerful framework for building agentic AI applicationsIt simplifies the creation of multi-agent systems by providing primitives such as:: LLM’s equipped with Instructions & Tools.: Allows delegating a specific task to another agent. Suitable for multi-agent settings.: Safety nets are used to validate inputs to agents. Talk about security.Designed for flexibility and scalability, the SDK allows developers to orchestrate workflows, integrate tools, and debug processes seamlessly.One of the standout points is OpenAI Agent SDK don’t have a steep learning curve and allows building complex system or task automation a breeze.The SDK has two driving design principles:Enough features to be worth using, but few enough primitives to make it quick to learn.Works great out of the box, but you can customize exactly what happens.Let’s explore how simple it is to get started by creating a hello world program - Haiku on Python!
Haiku On Python - hello world program! 💡 To keep things organized, create a new environment and enable it.Install the package using:Create a file agent.py and. env in the and paste the following code:Import necessary tools like Runner and Agent primitive class.Initializes the Agent name and instruction (think system prompt, but diff)Calles the Runner method to execute task synchronously i.e asking agent to “write a haiku about python”.In env file add your *OPENAI_API_KEY = "your_api_key" and run the program with:*Amazing, we just made an AI agent in just 4 lines of code, pretty wild right!However, there is more to OpenAI Agent SDK feature offerings and why it can be a game changer. Let’s look at few of them.
Key Features of Open AI Agents SDK
OpenAI Agents SDK Features, credits (adasci.org)Though there are a lot of hidden features that we are yet to explore, here are key ones:: A built-in mechanism that handles calling tools, sending results to the language model and continuing the loop until completion. (feel like Cursor): Leverages native Python features for orchestration rather than introducing new abstractions, reducing the learning curve. (fast and easy to get started): Enables coordination and delegation between multiple agents, allowing for specialized task handling. (co-working): Provides input validation and safety checks that run parallel to your agents and can break execution early if checks fail. (security net): Converts any Python function into a tool with automatic schema generation and validation. (function calling automation): Built-in visualization tools for debugging and monitoring workflows, plus integration with OpenAI's evaluation and fine-tuning capabilities. (visualization support)In essence, Agents SDK offers several standout features that make it a strong choice for AI Projects. But does it stand out in the case of complex projects?
Building with OpenAI Agents SDK & Composio We will use Composio tools integration and OpenAI Agent SDK to simplify building and develop agents. But before that, *Composio is an agentic integration platform that enables LLMs/AI agents to interact with external services via function calling. Its rich set of tools allows seamless integrations and reduces burden on developers.*We will use a virtual environment to keep things isolated. I am using Windows, so some commands might differ for other OSs. Start by creating a new folder and opening it in your editor of choice. I am going with Cursor.Next open the terminal and create a virtual env with:Make sure to replace with any meaningful name,Now activate the environment with:composio-learncriptsctivate In this case, is my environment name. For Linux/ max, refer online.Finally, let’s add all the necessary libraries with:pip composio-openai openai-agents next, we need to set up composio. Follow the steps to setup composio successfully:Head to Composio.dev and sign in/sign up for the account. It's recommended that you use GitHub for the job.Next, install an alternative to pip for package management. This is done as composio requires uvx for setup:curl https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh
Now login to composio using:uvx composio-core composio login If done correctly, it will open a browser with an access key, which you need to paste in the terminal. Once done, the key will automatically be generated in the backend.uvx composio-core composio As the API key is visible, save it in the environment variable using: .env
YOUR_API_KEY Next , authenticate the cli & GitHub using:& follow the instructions in the CLI to authenticate and connect your GitHub account.Once we are done, we will get started!
Writing Code for Starred GitHub Agent To keep things simple, we will test the agent on a single functionality: Star, a GitHub repo provided by the user in the prompt. Here is the base code to get started:Intimidating, right? Let’s break it down!Imports necessary modules, including OpenAI, Composio, and custom agent components.Loads environment variables (, , , ) and validates them.Initializes OpenAI client and disables tracing for debugging.Sets up Composio toolset to enable GitHub repository starring (as per our use case).Defines a custom model provider to fetch AI models dynamically.Implements star_github_repo(repo_name: str) to star a GitHub repository using OpenAI and Composio.Creates an AI agent () with instructions to star repositories.Runs the agent with a test command and prints the result.Uses to execute the script asynchronously.Now let’s see the agent in actionMake sure to replace "https://github.com/<user_name>/<repo_name>" in test section with an actual public GitHub Repo URL. I have changed mine too.Now head to the terminal & type:and see the magic happen!If you don’t see your repo starred🌟, refresh your browser, and it will be fixed.Having hands-on experience with building using Open AI Agents SDK, let’s look at how it compares against all its competitors- (the main section of the blog)
Comparing Agent Frameworks This section focuses on the comparison between OpenAI Agents vs LangGraph vs Autogen and CrewAI against various pointers like:Getting Started - Hello World docsFor ease of understanding, I have included a table for most of the pointers. So, let’s start with 1st comparison.Getting Started ExperienceHigh, with clear examplesVery simple (few lines of code)Comprehensive but technicalMore complex, requires understanding graph conceptsPractical with many examplesSimple with intuitive conceptsExtensive with diverse examplesI tested out all of them and here is my experience:: Super easy to start—just a few lines of code! The docs are well-written and not overwhelming. Enjoyed building with it.: Tough to begin with. Had to learn about graphs and states just for a simple agent. Docs are technical, not beginner-friendly—but they do offer a free course, which helped.: The second easiest. Requires understanding Tools and Roles, but the docs are well-structured and beginner-friendly.: A bit tricky. Needs manual setup and works around chat conversations. Confusing versioning in the docs made things harder.High with minimal abstractionsSeamless with function toolsSimple primitives for complex workflowsVery high with graph structuresStrong via LangChain integrationExcellent for complex, cyclical workflowHigh with role-based designNatural for team-based workflowsHigh with conversable agentsStrong with diverse toolsGood for conversation-based workflowsI tested out all of them for building complex agents, and here’s how they compare:: Highly customizable without adding unnecessary layers. Tool integration is seamless, and it uses simple primitives to build complex workflows.: Offers deep customization through graph structures. Best suited for cyclical workflows, but the learning curve is steep. Strong LangChain integration is a plus.: Uses a role-based approach, making it easy to design multi-agent systems. Tool integration is good, and it naturally fits team-based workflows.: Designed for conversation-based agents. Supports diverse tools, but its complexity lies in managing interactions rather than logic flow.Excellent with graph edgesSophisticated with state transitionsGood support via graph structureIntuitive with crew metaphorNatural with role-based designSupported with task managementExcellent with conversation patternsFlexible with agent topologiesSupported with agent networksI tested all of them for multi-agent support, and here’s how they stack up:: Strong in agent handoffs but lacks built-in parallel execution. Orchestration is simple and Python-based, making it easy to implement.: Excels in communication via graph edges, allowing sophisticated state transitions. Its graph structure makes parallel execution smoother.: Uses an intuitive "crew" metaphor, making agent communication natural. Orchestration is role-based, and task management helps with parallel execution.: Best for conversation-driven agents. Supports flexible agent topologies and enables parallel execution through agent networks.Good with built-in tracingExcellent with visualization tools2Excellent with graph stateSophisticated with conditional edgesGood with LangSmith integration710Simple with sequential/parallel processesFlexible with conversation patternsGood with conversation trackingState management refers to tracking the system's condition at any moment—an essential part of system design and a key pillar of any product.I tested it with long conversations, multiple interactions, and mid-session debugging. Here’s what I found:: State persistence is solid with built-in tracing. Simple function-based state transitions make it easy to work with, and debugging is excellent thanks to visualization tools.: Excels in managing state through graph structures. Transitions are sophisticated, leveraging conditional edges, and debugging is well-supported via LangSmith integration.: Handles basic state persistence through task outputs. Transitions are straightforward with sequential and parallel processes, but debugging options are limited.: Maintains agent memory well, making it suitable for conversation-driven workflows. State transitions are flexible, and debugging is supported with conversation tracking.High (designed for production)Large (part of LangChain)Excellent with LangChain ecosystemMedium-High (designed for production)Good with Python ecosystemA strong ecosystem and support help developers fix issues and stay updated.I often get stuck on small things—thankfully, communities exist! A good ecosystem is always a plus.Let’s see how these frameworks compare:: Still new but growing fast. Strong integration with OpenAI tools and built for production use.: Benefits from LangChain’s large ecosystem. Excellent integration within that space, making it a solid choice for complex workflows.: Has a growing community and fits well into the Python ecosystem. Suitable for production (only crew-based workflows) but still maturing.: Backed by Microsoft, with a large community and strong tool integration. Production readiness is solid but not yet at the highest level.It seems like everyone have their quirks, so it's better to do an overall comparison and here are the results (In table format):Lightweight with minimal abstractions, quick to learnSteepest, requires designing agentic architectureModerate, requires understanding of agents, tasksFastest, easy to get startedFlexible with customizable agents, tools, and workflowsMost flexible, supports any agentic architectureMore flexible than AutoGen, primarily task-basedLeast flexible, based on actor frameworkCompatible with any model supporting Chat Completions API formatIntegrates with Python, JavaScript, and LangChainDirect LangChain integration, supports monitoringGood LLM integrations, strong with MicrosoftScalability & DeployabilityDesigned for production-ready applications with built-in tracing and debuggingHighly scalable with Pragal and Apache BeamMore scalable than AutoGen, supports async executionScalable with async messaging, shifting architectureComprehensive with examples and tutorialsImproved, includes courses but still challengingExcellent, structured with guides and blogsGood, but discovery could be improvedHuman-in-the-Loop SupportConfigurable safety checks with guardrails for input and output validationVery flexible, intervention at any pointOnly at the end of a taskLimited (never, always, or at termination)First-class support for streamingSupports streaming but setup is complexNo dedicated low-code interfaceLangGraph Studio (custom IDE, not truly low-code)No official low-code UI (unofficial exists)AutoGen Studio (open-source low-code tool)Built-in tracing for visualizing and debugging agent executionFull time travel support for exploring alternativesSupports time travel from last crew runRequires explicit state management implementationBuilt-in support for short-term, long-term, user memory The OpenAI Agents SDK is a recent release, and some features may evolve as the platform matures. For the most current information, refer to the official documentation.Also, I found this comment under one of videos, I was researching:Though I would like to share my experience as Final Verdict.I know it might not align with yours, but after comparing all frameworks across multiple dimensions, here's my recommendation:Choose OpenAI Agents SDK if:You want a simple, production-ready framework with minimal learning curve.You're already using OpenAI models and want tight integration.You need strong tracing and debugging capabilities, baked into the SDK, enabled via a one-line command.You prefer Python-native approaches with few abstractions.Preferred option for productionYou need complex, cyclical workflows with sophisticated state management.You're building applications with multiple interdependent agents.You're already familiar with the LangChain ecosystem.You need visual representation of your agent workflows.Ready to learn / already have prior experience in coding and graph structure and theory.You want the most intuitive approach to multi-agent systems.You prefer thinking in terms of roles, goals, and tasks.You need a framework that naturally models human team structures.You want a balance between simplicity and power.Not going for production.You need flexible conversation patterns between agents.You're building applications with diverse conversational agent.You want strong support from Microsoft's research team.You need a framework that's been battle-tested in research settings.Ultimately, the best framework depends on your specific use case, team expertise, and project requirements. Here is a simple flow chart to get started (ignore swarm & n8n)The good news is that all four frameworks are actively developed, well-documented, and capable of building powerful AI agent systems. As the field evolves, we can expect these frameworks to continue improving and potentially converging on common patterns and best practices.What's your experience with these frameworks? Have you built something interesting with any of them? Share your thoughts in the comments below!

item.title: LeetCode 993: Cousins in Binary Tree – DFS and BFS Approaches Explained (Python)
item.author: Mehemmed
item.content:encoded:
LeetCode 993: Cousins in Binary Tree – DFS and BFS Explained (Python) In this post, I’ll walk through my two solutions to LeetCode Problem 993: , using both a approach and an * approach. Each method has its own strengths, and I explain how I used them to solve the problem cleanly in Python.Two nodes of a binary tree are cousins if they have the same depth but different parents.Given the root of a binary tree with unique values, and the values of two different nodes and , return if they are cousins, otherwise.It is guaranteed that nodes with the value and exists, so in each of the 2 solutions we should make sure that those nodes' parents and depths are tracked and compared correctly
First approach DFS: Depth First Search We define helper function called which recursively searches for the node first in , if not found, function searches it in . When the node is found its depth and parent is returnedAt the end it compares their depth and parent according to the problem statement such that it will return if their depth are equal and parents are different, otherwise.
Second Approach: Breadth-First Search Overall logic is similar but this time we traverse the tree such that nodes in the same depth are visited first, which makes sense to do if we are looking for cousins.Perfect for checking whether two nodes are at the , and have .Writing out both helped me understand how traversal methods affect problem-solving in trees — and I recommend trying both when you study similar problems.If you liked this breakdown, feel free to follow me. I’ll be sharing more clean solutions and dev thoughts regularly.

item.title: Master Python Automation: Extract and Display File Info Like a Pro
item.author: Jude Wakim
item.content:encoded:
“Your company needs to learn about the files located on various machines. You have been asked to build a script that extracts information such as the name and size about the files in the current working directory and stores it in a list of dictionaries.” -LUIT, Python2Automation scripting is a game-changer and the LUIT-Python2 GitHub repository provides a robust solution. This repository offers a Python-based tool that simplifies tasks like network provisioning, application deployment, and system configuration.
Why Automate Infrastructure Management? By automating repetitive tasks, you save time and reduce the risk of human error. Each infrastructure deployment is identical, ensuring that “it works on my machine” never becomes an issue. Automated scripts can scale effortlessly, provisioning multiple environments with just a few commands. Once an automated script is written, it can be reused, updated, and version-controlled.Before diving into the repository, make sure you have Python 2.7 (or compatible) installed on your machine. You will also need a basic understanding of Linux-based systems, as many of the scripts are tailored for that environment. Start by cloning the LUIT-Python2 repository from GitHub:git clone https://github.com/Judewakim/LUIT-Python2.git The repository uses a set of Python packages to handle various tasks. You can install them using pip:cd LUIT-Python2 pip install -r requirements.txtTo break down the essential components of the data_extraction.py file, lets start with the imports. This code requires to import information about the operating system and the datetime.Next, we begin defining the first function of the code. This function will collect all the information that we will need. Remember, the goal of this code is to “build a script that extracts information such as the name and size about the files in the current working directory” so we will collect all the information about the working directory (or whatever directory is specified) and later we will present this information similar to the ls -al command in the Linux command line. That function will look like this:def get_files_info(path='.'): # Function that collects file details, defaulting to current directory files_info = [] # List to store file details
for root, _, files in os.walk(path): # Recursively traverse directories and files for filename in files: file_path = os.path.join(root, filename) # Construct the full file path file_stat = os.stat(file_path) # Get file details/statistics
files_info.append({ # Store file details in a dictionary 'name': filename, # File name 'path': file_path, # Full file path 'size_bytes': file_stat.st_size, # File size in bytes 'last_modified': time.ctime(file_stat.st_mtime), # Last modified time 'permissions': oct(file_stat.st_mode)[-3:], # File permissions in octal format (last 3 digits) })
return files_info # Return the list of file details Now, we have a function that collects the file name, file path, file size, last modification time, and file permissions of each file in the path. The next thing to do is create another function that will display all this collected data in the way we want it. That second function will look like this:def print_ll_view(files_info): # Function to print file details in 'll' view format for file in files_info: # Loop through the list of file dictionaries print(f"{file['permissions']} {file['size_bytes']} {file['last_modified']} {file['path']}") # Print file details Lastly, we will call this functions and add a bit of user interaction to make the program more streamlined for the user. That last bit of code is set to only run when the Python is run directly and cannot be called from another file. This is what that looks like:if __name__ == "__main__": # Run the script only if executed directly path = input("Enter the directory path (press Enter to use current directory): ") or "." # Prompt user for path, default to current directory files_data = get_files_info(path) # Get file details for the specified path print("\nLinux CLI 'll' View:") # Print header print_ll_view(files_data) # Display file details The program is created and ready to be used. Navigate to the location where the program is stored. If you cloned it from my Github repository the location should be LUIT-Python2 . Once there, you can run the program use the command python . At this point, you have a Python program that will display the files from whatever location you specify and will display those files in Linux ls format for easy readability. This program can be repeated with whatever file path you need.You can modify this program to fit your company’s needs by cloning it locally or forking it on Github.This entire project is available on GitHub

item.title: Signals in Python: How Angular's Favorite Magic Brings Life to Zombie Backends
item.author: buiapp
item.content:encoded:
A Spooky Tale of State Management (With Angular's Magic) Remember when JavaScript state management was scarier than an abandoned amusement park? Around 2010, Knockout.js haunted developers with reactive observables, whispering: Since then, state management evolved dramatically:: Angular 1.x and its infamous dirty-checking nightmares.: React’s relentless re-rendering—AKA the zombie apocalypse strategy.: SolidJS resurrected signals, elegantly slicing through dependency trees.: Angular 16 fully embraced signals—bringing the prodigal son home with style.Yet, Python backends remained stuck in the twilight zone:Backend developers turned into exhausted ghostbusters, manually rattling callback chains. Until now...
Summoning Angular Magic with Angular developers love signals because they effortlessly manage dependencies and state changes. Now, Python devs can finally share the magic:
Meet Angular's Ghostly Python Cousins: - The Reactive Beacon Automatically broadcasts changes. - The Dependency Alchemist Magically recalculates when dependencies change. - The Reactive Guardian Responds instantly to state shifts.
Why Beats Manual Callback Rituals Old Nightmare (The Callback Curse)New Angular-Style Reactivity in PythonKey Angular-like advantages:✅ Automatic dependency management
Real-Life Resurrection: A Job Management Example Imagine an app that requires controlling long-running tasks, including live progress and user-driven start/pause:
Angular-Style Job Management in Python
Why Angular Devs Will Love in Python Automatic Dependency Tracking: Like Angular, changes propagate without manual triggers.: Thread-safe, reducing concurrency nightmares.Efficient Resource Management: Automatic cleanup prevents memory leaks.: Complex reactive flows become simple.If you love Angular signals, you'll feel right at home with . It’s finally safe to let your backend state management rest in peace."The power of Angular signals, now haunting Python backends too!"Your backend’s Angular-powered resurrection begins now...

item.title: Struggling with Presenting My Graph-Based Recommendation System
item.author: Mohammed Favas
item.content:encoded: I'm working on a graph-based video recommendation system, inspired by a research paper. Unlike traditional Collaborative Filtering (CF) or Content-Based Filtering (CBF), my approach models user interactions as a graph, allowing for a more structured and scalable way to determine recommendations.However, when presenting my project, I faced skepticism. People questioned my claims about scalability and effectiveness, and I realized that just stating it's better isn’t enough—I need stronger data-backed arguments.How My Recommendation System WorksThe system operates in five main steps:Building a User-Video Graph: Users are connected to the videos they've watched, forming a bipartite graph.Building a Video Similarity Graph: Videos are connected based on shared audience patterns, forming a weighted similarity network.Clustering the Videos: I use modularity-based clustering (Leiden algorithm) to group similar videos.Ranking Videos Using Centrality Metrics: Each video is ranked using Degree, Betweenness, Closeness, Aggregate, and Ego-Focused Centrality to determine its importance in the network.Generating Personalized Recommendations.The system recommends videos based on the user's cluster and centrality scores.The main intuition behind this is that if users naturally form watching behavior clusters, then videos that are central in those clusters should make good recommendations.When I presented this, I compared it against CF and CBF in a table, explaining why graph-based approaches are more scalable. I argued that since everything is modeled as a graph, migrating to a graph database or cloud-based system should be more seamless."Anyone can claim something is scalable, but without numbers, it doesn’t mean much."And honestly, they’re not wrong. I need quantifiable evidence to show:Why a graph-based approach is easier to scale than CF/CBF.How I can visually present this in a way that makes the advantage clear.Whether there are any standard performance benchmarks for this type of comparison.Has anyone worked on a similar graph-based recommendation approach? What are some good ways to compare and validate its performance against CF/CBF? Any insights on how to present this data in a way that’s convincing to technical and non-technical audiences?Would love to hear your thoughts!

item.title: Comment l'intelligence artificielle a sauvé la vie d'un homme
item.author: Adam ALem
item.content:encoded:
L'intelligence artificielle (IA) est devenue un atout majeur dans de nombreux domaines, y compris celui de la santé. Dans cet article, nous allons explorer un cas poignant où l'IA a joué un rôle crucial dans la vie d'un homme en lui sauvant littéralement la vie. Découvrons ensemble comment la technologie intelligente a été le héros méconnu de cette histoire captivante.
L'introduction d'une technologie salvatrice L'histoire incroyable que nous allons partager met en lumière le potentiel révolutionnaire de l'intelligence artificielle dans le domaine médical. L'IA, dotée de capacités d'analyse et de prédiction avancées, a permis de diagnostiquer de manière précoce une maladie grave chez cet homme et a ainsi ouvert la voie à un traitement rapide et efficace, sauvant ainsi sa vie.
Le diagnostic précoce grâce à l'IA Grâce à ses algorithmes sophistiqués et à sa capacité à traiter d'énormes quantités de données, l'IA a identifié des schémas subtils dans les examens médicaux de l'homme qui auraient échappé à l'œil même des médecins les plus expérimentés. En comparant les résultats des tests avec une vaste base de données, l'IA a repéré des anomalies significatives qui ont alerté l'équipe médicale sur la présence d'une pathologie potentiellement mortelle.
Un plan de traitement sur mesure Une fois le diagnostic posé, l'IA a également contribué à élaborer un plan de traitement personnalisé en analysant les caractéristiques uniques de la maladie de l'homme et en prévoyant les réponses possibles aux différentes options thérapeutiques. Cette approche individualisée a permis d'optimiser l'efficacité du traitement et d'améliorer les chances de guérison du patient.
Surveillance continue et ajustements en temps réel L'IA n'a pas simplement livré son verdict et disparu. Elle a continué à surveiller l'évolution de l'état de santé de l'homme en temps réel, permettant ainsi de détecter toute complication potentielle ou de signaler des signes précurseurs d'amélioration. Cette capacité d'adaptation constante a été essentielle pour garantir un suivi attentif et ajuster le traitement en fonction des besoins changeants du patient.
La conclusion époustouflante : un sauvetage inattendu Grâce à l'intelligence artificielle et à la collaboration étroite entre la technologie et les professionnels de santé, l'homme a non seulement survécu à sa maladie mais a également retrouvé une qualité de vie optimale. Cette histoire poignante illustre le pouvoir transformateur de l'IA dans le domaine de la médecine, offrant de nouveaux espoirs et des solutions innovantes là où il n'y avait autrefois que des défis apparemment insurmontables.En conclusion, l'intelligence artificielle ne se contente pas d'être un outil technologique de plus, elle est devenue un partenaire précieux dans la lutte contre les maladies graves et les situations d'urgence médicale. L'histoire de cet homme est un témoignage vibrant de la manière dont l'IA peut réellement sauver des vies et ouvrir la voie à un avenir où la technologie et l'humanité se complètent pour le bien de tous.Intégrant des mots-clés SEO pertinents tels que "intelligence artificielle dans la santé", "diagnostic médical précoce", "technologie médicale avancée", cet article apporte non seulement un contenu informatif et captivant, mais également une visibilité accrue sur les moteurs de recherche pour quiconque s'intéresse aux avancées technologiques dans le domaine médical.

item.title: L’intelligence artificielle aux commandes d’un avion de chasse F-16
item.author: Adam ALem
item.content:encoded:
L’intelligence artificielle aux commandes d’un avion de chasse F-16 : vers une nouvelle ère de la technologie aérienneDans le domaine de l'aviation militaire, l'intégration de l'intelligence artificielle aux commandes d'un avion de chasse F-16 suscite à la fois fascination et questionnements. Cette avancée technologique promet de révolutionner la manière dont les avions de combat sont pilotés et opérés, ouvrant ainsi la voie à une ère nouvelle et passionnante de la technologie aérienne.
L'intelligence artificielle embarquée dans un F-16 : Comment ça fonctionne ? L'intelligence artificielle (IA) appliquée à un avion de chasse F-16 repose sur des algorithmes sophistiqués capables d'analyser en temps réel une multitude de données liées à l'environnement, à la mission et au vol lui-même. Ces algorithmes permettent à l'IA de prendre des décisions rapides et précises, dépassant en certains cas les capacités humaines en matière de réactivité et de traitement des informations.Mots-clés SEO : intelligence artificielle, avion de chasse, F-16, technologie aérienne, algorithmes, analyse de données, décisions rapides.
Avantages de l'IA aux commandes d'un F-16 Réduction du temps de réaction : L'intelligence artificielle permet aux avions de chasse d'anticiper certaines situations et de réagir plus rapidement face aux menaces.Optimisation des performances : Grâce à des calculs complexes, l'IA peut ajuster en temps réel les paramètres de vol pour maximiser l'efficacité.Réduction de la charge de travail des pilotes : En prenant en charge certaines tâches, l'IA permet aux pilotes de se concentrer sur des aspects tactiques et stratégiques.
Défis et limites de cette technologie révolutionnaire Bien que l'intelligence artificielle aux commandes d'un F-16 présente de nombreux avantages, plusieurs défis et limites persistent. La fiabilité des systèmes autonomes, la protection contre les cyber-attaques ou encore l'éthique des décisions prises par l'IA sont autant de questions cruciales à adresser.
Conclusion : vers un avenir prometteur L'intégration de l'intelligence artificielle aux commandes d'un avion de chasse F-16 ouvre la voie à de nouvelles possibilités et défis dans le domaine de l'aviation militaire. À mesure que la technologie évolue, il est essentiel de garantir un développement responsable et éthique de l'IA, en veillant à ce qu'elle contribue à renforcer la sécurité et l'efficacité des opérations aériennes.En définitive, l'utilisation de l'intelligence artificielle dans l'aéronautique représente un pas incontournable vers une modernisation des pratiques et des technologies, propulsant ainsi l'aviation militaire vers un avenir à la fois prometteur et stimulant.Mots-clés SEO : aviation militaire, technologie évolue, sécurité, efficacité, aéronautique, modernisation des pratiques.
