item.title: 🔍 ContentLens | AI-Powered Document Insights
item.author: Vikas Awasthi
item.content:encoded:
Building ContentLens: My Journey Creating an AI-Powered Document Processing App Over the past weekend, I embarked on an exciting project to build ContentLens - a web application that uses AI to analyze and transform documents. In this blog post, I'll share my experience building this application, the technologies I used, challenges I faced, and what I learned along the way.ContentLens is a simple yet powerful application that:Accepts various document formats (text, markdown, JSON, DOCX, and images)Processes them using Google's Gemini AIReturns the results in markdown format that you can downloadWhether you need to summarize a long document, extract key points, translate content, or transform it into a different format, ContentLens can help. The application is designed with simplicity and privacy in mind - all uploaded files are processed and immediately deleted.For this project, I chose to work with:: These frameworks provided a clean way to build server-rendered interfaces with minimal JavaScript: As the backend language, handling file processing and API integration: For the AI capabilities that power the document analysis: For deployment and hosting
Building the Application: Step by Step
1. Planning the Architecture I began by planning a clean object-oriented architecture with these main components:Document class for handling different file typesProcessor class for interacting with the Gemini APIWeb routes for handling user requests
2. File Processing Challenges One of the more challenging aspects was handling different file types. Each format required a different approach:Text and markdown files needed simple readingDOCX files required parsing with python-docxImages needed special handling for the AII implemented a strategy pattern where the Document class would handle extraction differently based on file type.
3. Privacy and Security Considerations From the beginning, I wanted to ensure user privacy. I implemented:Immediate deletion of uploaded files after processingRemoval of processed results after downloadEnvironment variables for API keysInput validation and error handling
4. User Experience Enhancements Based on feedback from early testers, I added:Processing status feedbackHelpful example instructionsThis project taught me several valuable lessons:The power of separation of concerns: By keeping document handling, AI processing, and web interfaces separate, the code remained clean and maintainable.The importance of user feedback: Adding visual indicators for uploads and processing made the application much more user-friendly.Deployment considerations: Ensuring environment variables were properly set up in Railway and that file paths worked correctly in the deployed environment.The value of iterative development: Starting with a minimal viable product and adding features based on feedback proved effective.While ContentLens is functional, there are several enhancements I'm considering:Support for more file formats (PDF, EPUB)Batch processing of multiple filesCustom AI model selectionUser accounts for saving processing historyAdditional output formats beyond markdownI welcome any feedback or suggestions for improvement!
What I Learned & What's Next Building ContentLens taught me a lot about integrating AI APIs and creating clean architecture. I'm planning to add PDF support and batch processing next.What other file formats would you find useful in a tool like this?Have you worked with the Gemini API? How does it compare to other LLMs?What challenges have you faced when deploying Python web apps?I'd love to hear your thoughts and suggestions in the comments!

item.title: পাইথন এরর হ্যান্ডলিং এবং ডিবাগিং: সমস্যা মোকাবিলা করা
item.author: Shafi Rayhan
item.content:encoded:
পাইথন শেখার জার্নিতে এসে আরেকটা গুরুত্বপূর্ণ ধাপে পৌঁছেছি! আজ আমরা এরর হ্যান্ডলিং এবং ডিবাগিং নিয়ে কথা বলব। কোড লিখতে গেলে ভুল হবেই—মূল কথা হলো সেগুলো কীভাবে সামলাবেন। এই পোস্টে আপনি শিখবেন:, , , এবং ব্লক ব্যবহার।বিভিন্ন ধরনের এরর এবং কাস্টম এরর।একটা প্রজেক্ট যেখানে ইনপুট চেক করব (যেমন ইমেইল)।এরর হ্যান্ডলিং শিখলে আপনার প্রোগ্রাম ক্র্যাশ করবে না এবং ডিবাগিং শিখলে ভুল খুঁজে ঠিক করা সহজ হবে। চলুন শুরু করি!প্রোগ্রামে এরর হলে সেটা ধরে সঠিকভাবে সামলানোই হলো এরর হ্যান্ডলিং। পাইথনে দিয়ে এটা করা হয়।, , , এবং : এখানে সমস্যা হতে পারে এমন কোড লেখা হয়।: এরর হলে কী করতে হবে তা বলে।: এরর হোক বা না হোক, এটা সবসময় চলবে।: এরর না হলে এটা চলবে।ইনপুট যদি “abc” হয়: ধরবে।ইনপুট যদি “5” হয়: চলবে।একটা সংখ্যা লিখুন: 5 আপনি লিখেছেন: 5 প্রোগ্রাম শেষ।
বিভিন্ন ধরনের এরর এবং কাস্টম এরর : ভুল টাইপের ইনপুট (যেমন )।: ফাইল না পাওয়া গেলে।: শূন্য দিয়ে ভাগ করলে।: লিস্টে ভুল ইনডেক্স ব্যবহার।নিজের এরর তৈরি করতে ব্যবহার করা যায়:বয়স -৫ হলে: উঠবে। নিজের শর্ত অনুযায়ী এরর তৈরি করতে।ডিবাগিং মানে কোডের ভুল খুঁজে ঠিক করা।: কোডে কী হচ্ছে দেখতে। : ছোট অংশ চালিয়ে দেখুন।: সমস্যার জায়গা বাদ দিয়ে চালান।:
ব্রেকপয়েন্ট সেট করে কোড ধাপে ধাপে চালান।ভ্যারিয়েবলের মান দেখতে পারেন।: কমান্ড লাইনে ডিবাগ করা। ভুল দ্রুত খুঁজে ঠিক করতে।
প্রজেক্ট: ইমেইল চেকার প্রোগ্রাম চলুন একটা প্রোগ্রাম বানাই যেটা ইমেইল ঠিক আছে কি না চেক করবে।নতুন ফাইল বানান, নাম দিন । আপনার ইমেইল লিখুন (বা 'exit' লিখে বের হন): abc ভুল: ইমেইল-এ @ এবং . থাকতে হবে! চেক শেষ।
আপনার ইমেইল লিখুন (বা 'exit' লিখে বের হন): rahim@example.com ইমেইল ঠিক আছে: rahim@example.com চেক শেষ। : ইমেইল চেক করে, ভুল হলে এরর উঠায়।: ভুল ধরে মেসেজ দেখায়।: প্রতিবার চেক শেষে চলে।: লুপ থেকে বের হওয়ার উপায়।এই প্রজেক্টে এরর হ্যান্ডলিং-এর শক্তি বোঝা যায়।
হোমওয়ার্ক: লগইন সিস্টেম সিমুলেশন আপনার কাজ হলো একটা লগইন সিস্টেম বানানো যেটা ভুল ইনপুটের জন্য এরর মেসেজ দেবে।ফাইল বানান, নাম দিন । ইউজারনেম লিখুন: ria পাসওয়ার্ড লিখুন: wrong লগইন ব্যর্থ: পাসওয়ার্ড ভুল! ইউজারনেম লিখুন: rahim পাসওয়ার্ড লিখুন: pass123 লগইন সফল! স্বাগতম, rahim! : ডিকশনারিতে ইউজারনেম-পাসওয়ার্ড।: ইনপুট চেক করে, ভুল হলে এরর উঠায়।: ভুল ধরে মেসেজ দেখায়।: Ctrl+C চাপলে বন্ধ হয়।, , , দিয়ে এরর হ্যান্ডলিং।বিভিন্ন এরর এবং কাস্টম এরর তৈরি।এরর হ্যান্ডলিং এবং ডিবাগিং শিখে আপনি এখন আরও ভালো প্রোগ্রামার। প্রজেক্ট আর হোমওয়ার্ক চেষ্টা করুন। কমেন্টে বলুন কেমন লাগলো বা কোথায় সমস্যা হয়েছে।এরর হ্যান্ডলিং কি বোঝা সহজ লেগেছে?লগইন সিস্টেমে কি কিছু যোগ করতে চান?ডিবাগিং-এ কোন টেকনিক বেশি পছন্দ হয়েছে?

item.title: [Part 7]File I/O and Data Persistence in Python for QA Automation
item.author: TestAmplify
item.content:encoded: Reading and writing data is a key part of test automation. Whether it’s parsing logs, storing test results, or using CSV and JSON files, this module will help you master Python’s file-handling features.Lesson 1: Reading and Writing Text Files Text files help store logs, config values, and test data persistently. Basic file operations., , , Using to manage file resources. Always use for safer file handling.Lesson 2: Working with CSV Files CSV files are widely used in data-driven testing to store and read test cases and results.csv.reader and csv.writer: Standard Python CSV tools.DictReader and DictWriter: Work with CSV rows as dictionaries. Looping through test datasets. Use CSV files for quick and readable input data during test case execution.Lesson 3: Parsing JSON Data JSON is a flexible format used in API automation and configuration files.json.load() / json.dump(): Reading and writing JSON files.json.loads() / json.dumps(): Parsing strings in-memory. Validating API responses, storing test data. Keep your JSON schemas clean and predictable for easy debugging.Lesson 4: Introduction to File System Operations Python’s and modules help manage test directories and files.Creating/Deleting Folders:, , Use for safe temporary file storage during test runs.Lesson 5: Best Practices for Handling Large Test Data Files Efficient file I/O becomes important when dealing with large datasets. Avoid memory overload. Using gzip. When files become unwieldy. Always close large files or use to manage memory efficiently.Python provides powerful tools to handle file I/O, making it easy to store, retrieve, and manipulate test data.Use text files for logs and results.Use CSV for readable, structured data inputs.Use JSON for config and API test data.Use and to organize automation directories and output. In the final module, we’ll explore Python for Selenium: Preparation for UI Automation, where you’ll get hands-on with browser automation using Python.

item.title: [Part 8]Introduction to Python for Selenium (Preparation for UI Automation)
item.author: TestAmplify
item.content:encoded: Selenium is the go-to library for browser automation. When paired with Python, it becomes a powerful tool for end-to-end UI testing. This module helps you get started with Selenium and understand its fundamentals.Lesson 1: Introduction to Selenium WebDriver Selenium controls browsers programmatically to simulate user interactions. Open-source tool for browser automation. WebDriver, Grid, IDE.Why Use Selenium with Python? Simplicity, readability, and community support. Start with WebDriver—it’s the most powerful and widely used component.Lesson 2: Setting up Selenium with Python Install Selenium, download a browser driver, and write your first automation script.Setting up ChromeDriver or GeckoDriver.Launching a Browser and Navigating to a Site. Use to auto-manage browser drivers.Lesson 3: Basic Web Element Interaction Learn to find and interact with web elements like buttons, text fields, and dropdowns., , , , , and Wait for elements to load using before interacting.Lesson 4: Understanding XPath and CSS Selectors Selecting elements accurately is crucial for reliable tests.//tag[@attribute='value'], , Choosing the most reliable selector. Use browser DevTools to test and refine your selectors.Python with Selenium provides everything needed to create robust UI automation scripts.Selenium automates browsers for UI testing.Install Selenium and a browser driver to get started.Use WebDriver methods to interact with web elements.Master XPath and CSS selectors for locating elements. With the fundamentals covered, you’re now ready to explore advanced Selenium features like waits, assertions, data-driven testing, and framework integration!

item.title: [Part 6]Error Handling and Exception Handling in Python for Robustness
item.author: TestAmplify
item.content:encoded: Robust test automation scripts gracefully handle unexpected errors. This module shows how to handle exceptions, raise custom errors, and debug scripts effectively using Python.Lesson 1: Understanding Errors and Exceptions Errors occur when something goes wrong in your script—either due to syntax, logic, or runtime issues.Syntax Errors vs Runtime Errors: Compile-time vs execution failures., , , etc. Preventing crashes and improving clarity. Always review tracebacks—they point directly to the issue.Lesson 2: Implementing try-except Blocks Python’s block allows you to catch and respond to errors without halting your test. Block of code to attempt. Handle a specific exception. Code that runs regardless of outcome. Run if no exception occurs. Use to close resources or perform cleanup.Lesson 3: Raising Custom Exceptions Define your own errors to signal specific conditions in your test scripts. Triggering errors manually.Creating Custom Exception Classes: Subclassing . Invalid inputs, failed validations, etc. Prefix custom exceptions with or for easy identification.Lesson 4: Logging and Debugging Python Scripts Logging provides a record of execution flow, and debugging tools help locate and fix errors efficiently. Write logs to console or file., , , , Insert with . Use inside except blocks to log stack traces.Exception handling ensures test scripts don’t crash unexpectedly and provides better control over failures.Use try-except to gracefully manage errors.Define custom exceptions for specific test conditions.Log important actions and exceptions to simplify debugging.Use debugging tools to pinpoint issues quickly. In the next module, you’ll learn about File I/O and Data Persistence in Python for QA Automation, including reading logs, writing reports, and managing test data.

item.title: [Part 4]Data Structures in Python: Organizing Test Data Effectively
item.author: TestAmplify
item.content:encoded: Organizing test data is critical for building scalable test automation frameworks. This module introduces key Python data structures—lists, dictionaries, sets, and tuples—and how to use them in test automation.Lesson 1: Lists in Python Lists are ordered, mutable collections used to manage sequences of test data. Using brackets [] Accessing elements and subsets. Adding, removing, sorting elements. Use list comprehensions for concise operations.Lesson 2: Dictionaries in Python Dictionaries store key-value pairs, making them ideal for test case metadata. Using curly braces {}Accessing and Updating Values: Modifying test data. Looping through keys and values. Use to safely retrieve dictionary values.Lesson 3: List and Dictionary Comprehensions Comprehensions simplify data processing into a single readable line.[expression for item in list]Dictionary Comprehensions:{key: value for item in list} Avoid using comprehensions for complex logic — keep it readable.Lesson 4: Working with Sets and Tuples Sets ensure uniqueness, and tuples are immutable collections useful in test logs. Unordered, unique elements. Immutable sequences. Removing duplicates, logging unchangeable test states. Convert lists to sets to eliminate duplicates in test case names.Lesson 5: Nested Data Structures Combining structures enables handling of complex test scenarios. Representing multiple test cases. Grouping data by categories. Keep nesting shallow for readability and easier debugging.Python’s data structures help organize, manage, and access test data efficiently.Lists manage ordered test steps.Dictionaries store named test data.Sets and tuples help enforce data integrity.Nested structures handle real-world test scenarios. In the next module, we’ll cover Functions and Modules: Writing Reusable and Modular Python QA Code to help you build maintainable test libraries.

item.title: [Part 1]Setting Up Your Python Environment for Test Automation
item.author: TestAmplify
item.content:encoded:
A well-configured Python environment is essential for efficient test automation. This module guides you through setting up Python, installing necessary tools, and configuring your development environment for smooth test automation workflows.Lesson 1: Introduction to Python and Its Relevance in Test Automation Python is a popular choice for test automation due to its simplicity, vast library support, and strong community. Why Python is widely used for automation. API testing, UI automation, and performance testing.Community and Library Support: Selenium, PyTest, and other frameworks. Python's readable syntax makes it easy for manual testers to transition into automation.Lesson 2: Installing Python and Essential Tools (pip, Virtual Environments) Setting up Python correctly ensures a stable test automation environment.Downloading and Installing Python: Installing the latest version from python.org. Managing dependencies for automation projects.Setting Up Virtual Environments: Isolating dependencies for different projects. python python ensurepip python venv test_env
test_env/bin/activate Always use virtual environments to avoid dependency conflicts between projects.Lesson 3: Setting Up Your Python Development Environment (IDE Choice & Configuration) Choosing the right IDE enhances productivity when writing test automation scripts. PyCharm, Visual Studio Code, Sublime Text.Installing Python Extensions: Configuring linters and debuggers.Auto-formatting and Linting: Using tools like and . pip black flake8
black my_script.py Enable auto-formatting in your IDE to maintain consistent code style.Lesson 4: Understanding Python's Interactive Interpreter and Script Execution Python’s interactive shell (REPL) and script execution help in debugging and quick testing.Using the Python Interpreter: Running Python commands interactively.Executing Python Scripts: Running files.Debugging with Python REPL: Quickly testing functions and logic. python
python test_script.py Use the interactive shell to test snippets before adding them to scripts.Lesson 5: Common Pitfalls and Best Practices When Setting Up Python for QA Avoiding common mistakes when setting up Python ensures a smoother experience in test automation. Ensuring consistency between Python versions. Keeping test libraries up-to-date.Handling Environment Variables: Managing API keys and configurations securely. pip list
pip selenium Use a file to manage project dependencies efficiently.This module covered the essential setup for using Python in test automation, from installation to IDE configuration and best practices.Python is a powerful and flexible choice for automation testing.Virtual environments prevent dependency conflicts.Choosing the right IDE boosts productivity.Understanding script execution and debugging is crucial for efficient development. In the next module, we will explore Python Fundamentals: Syntax, Data Types, and Operators for QA, covering the core programming concepts necessary for writing effective test automation scripts.

item.title: [Part 2]Python Fundamentals: Syntax, Data Types, and Operators for QA
item.author: TestAmplify
item.content:encoded: Understanding Python fundamentals is essential for writing clean, maintainable, and efficient test scripts. This module introduces core concepts such as syntax rules, key data types, and operators used in test automation.Lesson 1: Python Syntax Essentials Python relies on indentation and simplicity, making it beginner-friendly yet powerful.Indentation Instead of Braces: Consistent use of whitespace is critical.Single-Line & Multi-Line Comments: Adding context to your test logic. Writing executable scripts. Use 4 spaces per indentation level to maintain consistency.Lesson 2: Core Data Types in Python Data types help define the nature of your variables and enable test script logic. list, tuple, dict, set Use to check the data type of any variable.Lesson 3: Operators in Python Operators are used for calculations and comparisons in automation logic., , , , , , , , , , , , , Use for value comparison and for identity comparison.Lesson 4: Working with Strings in Python String operations are common in UI validation and log checks. Joining strings with Using f-strings or Extracting substrings using , , , , Use f-strings for readable and efficient string formatting.This module laid the foundation for writing Python automation scripts by covering the syntax, data types, and operators.Proper indentation is key to Python's syntax.Know your data types to make logical decisions in tests.Operators are essential for test validations.String manipulation helps with assertions and data validation. In the next module, we’ll explore Control Flow and Decision Making in Python for QA Scripts, which will help you implement conditional logic and loops in your test scripts.

item.title: [Part 3]Control Flow and Decision Making in Python for QA Scripts
item.author: TestAmplify
item.content:encoded: Control flow is the backbone of any decision-making in test automation. This module explores how to use conditionals and loops to add logic and repetition to your test scripts.Lesson 1: Conditional Statements (if, elif, else) Use conditional logic to determine the flow of your test cases. Executes a block if a condition is true. Checks other conditions if previous ones fail. Default action if no condition is met. Keep your conditional logic clean and readable.Lesson 2: Mastering Loops (for, while) Loops help automate repetitive test tasks such as running multiple test cases. Iterating over sequences like lists or strings. Running until a condition is no longer true. Controlling loop execution. Use when you need the index while iterating.Lesson 3: Boolean Logic and Comparison Operators Logical and comparison operations are crucial for building assertions., , , , , , , Multiple logic checks in one statement. Group conditions using parentheses for clarity.Lesson 4: Practical Exercises Reinforce your learning by applying control flow to real-world QA scenarios.Create a test loop that simulates 5 API response validations.Write a script that classifies a test as Pass/Fail based on input.Build a script that exits a loop once all tests return "Passed." Practice makes perfect — the more you use control structures, the more intuitive they become.Control flow allows you to build intelligent test scripts that can make decisions and iterate dynamically.Use conditionals to direct test execution.Loops are ideal for repetitive validation tasks.Boolean logic helps automate result analysis. In the next module, we’ll explore Data Structures in Python: Organizing Test Data Effectively, where you’ll learn how to manage lists, dictionaries, and sets in your test scripts.

item.title: How to Fix Shopee Email Verification Issues: A Quick Guide for Sellers
item.author: Mindlily
item.content:encoded:
Hey there! Trying to set up your Shopee seller account, but the verification email isn’t arriving? Or maybe it’s bouncing back as invalid? Tech glitches can be such a headache, right? Let me walk you through some easy fixes I’ve learned from my own experience！
Check Your Spam Folder (Yes, Seriously!) First things first: always check your spam or junk folder. I know it’s obvious, but I’ve lost count of how many times I’ve overlooked this. Shopee’s automated emails sometimes end up here due to overzealous spam filters. If you find it, mark the email as “Not Spam” to help future messages land in your inbox.
Verify Your Email Address for Typos Double-check the email you entered during registration. Did you mix up a lowercase “L” with a capital “I”? Accidentally type “.con” instead of “.com”? Even a tiny mistake can block verification. If unsure, copy-paste the address directly from your email provider’s settings.
Request a New Verification Email If the first email didn’t arrive, head back to Shopee’s registration page and click “Resend Verification.” Sometimes emails get delayed or lost. Wait a few minutes, then check your inbox again.
Try a Different Email Provider (Works for Me!) I’ve had issues with free email services in the past, so I switched to a professional email service that supports custom domains (like GetMX). Not only did verification emails start arriving instantly, but I can also create unlimited addresses to organize my accounts. If your current email isn’t working, this might be the solution.
Use a Temporary Email (But Just for Now) If you’re in a rush, Gmail or ProtonMail could work temporarily. Just note that Shopee might flag temporary emails for security reasons. It’s a quick fix, but not ideal for long-term use.If nothing works, reach out to Shopee’s customer support. They’re usually quick to help and can manually verify your account. Have your registration details ready!Email issues are frustrating, but they’re usually easy to fix once you know where to look.Of course, Shopee’s system can have glitches too, so don’t hesitate to ask for help!

item.title: 🏎️ Faster, Cheaper, Better: The Power of Model Routing
item.author: Mai Chi Bao
item.content:encoded:
In this blog post, I’ll introduce you to a powerful technique called and guide you through creating a model router using , a framework optimized for few-shot learning. We'll cover the concept, its benefits, and a step-by-step tutorial, including training and inference code. Finally, we'll deploy the model to Hugging Face for easy access.Why We Need Model Routing Creating a Model Router with SetFit
In the rapidly evolving field of artificial intelligence, efficiency and cost-effectiveness are paramount. is an innovative technique that directs user queries to the most appropriate model based on their complexity or nature. For instance, simple questions can be handled by lightweight models like GPT-4o mini, while more intricate queries are routed to robust models like GPT-4. This approach optimizes resource usage, reduces costs, and speeds up response times.In this post, we’ll explore how to implement model routing using , a framework that excels at few-shot fine-tuning of sentence transformers. With minimal labeled data, SetFit enables us to build an effective model router—perfect for scenarios where extensive datasets are unavailable.Model routing is the process of classifying and directing user queries to the most suitable model based on their characteristics. As explained in Huyen Chip’s blog:"One use case of predictive human preference is model routing. For example, if we know in advance that for a prompt, users will prefer Claude Instant’s response over GPT-4, and Claude Instant is cheaper/faster than GPT-4, we can route this prompt to Claude Instant. Model routing has the potential to increase response quality while reducing costs and latency."Here’s a practical example:: "What is the capital of France?" → Routed to GPT-4o mini.: "Analyze the time complexity of the merge sort algorithm." → Routed to GPT-4.This technique leverages predictive human preference to anticipate which model will deliver the best response, balancing quality, cost, and speed.
Why We Need Model Routing Model routing addresses several critical needs in AI systems:: Consider the pricing: GPT-4 costs approximately $10 per million tokens, while GPT-4o mini costs just $0.6 per million tokens. If a business routes 60% of its queries to GPT-4o mini, it could save up to $5.64 per million tokens (calculated as 60% × ($10 - $0.6)).: Lightweight models like GPT-4o mini have lower latency, providing faster responses for simpler queries and enhancing user experience.: By reserving powerful models for complex tasks, we avoid overburdening resources unnecessarily.For this task, classification models outperform complex NLP models (e.g., LLMs) because they are:: Outputs are straightforward and easy to monitor.: Training and inference are quick.: They perform well even with small datasets, especially with few-shot learning frameworks like SetFit.
Creating a Model Router with SetFit Let’s build a model router using , an efficient framework for few-shot fine-tuning of sentence transformers. According to the SetFit GitHub page:"SetFit achieves high accuracy with little labeled data—for instance, with only 8 labeled examples per class on the Customer Reviews sentiment dataset."Our goal is to route simple queries (e.g., "What is microservices?") to a lightweight model like GPT-4o mini and complex queries (e.g., "Tell me the difference between microservice and service-based architecture.") to a more powerful model like GPT-4. The dataset is small, designed for few-shot learning, and includes prompts in Vietnamese and English. It’s hosted on Hugging Face: mrzaizai2k/gpt_routing.Here’s a sample of the labeled data:"What is microservices?""What is the capital of France?""Write a Python function that calculates the factorial of a number.""Tell me the difference between microservice and service-based architecture.""What is White-box testing? [with multiple-choice options]""Analyze the time complexity of the merge sort algorithm.": Complex queries.We’ll use the embedding model sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2, which supports both Vietnamese and English. You can swap this for another embedding model depending on your language requirements.The function reads settings (e.g., dataset name, batch size) from a YAML file (gpt_routing_train_config.yaml). You’ll need to create this file with appropriate configurations.The model splits the dataset into training, validation, and test sets to ensure robust evaluation.Once trained, the model can classify new queries and route them accordingly. Here’s how to perform inference using the trained model:The output will be (simple query) or (complex query), guiding the routing decision.In this post, we’ve introduced , a technique that optimizes AI systems by directing queries to the most suitable model based on complexity. Using , we built a model router with a small dataset, leveraging few-shot learning for high accuracy. We covered the dataset preparation, training process, inference, and deployment to Hugging Face.Model routing offers tangible benefits—cost savings, faster responses, and efficient resource use—making it a valuable tool for businesses and developers. With the resources provided (dataset, code, and model), you can adapt this approach to your own use case. For further exploration, check out the SetFit documentation or experiment with different embedding models to suit your language needs.

GitHub Actions +6%Another weird thing is Jenkins is going up +6%Cloud certificates are getting mentioned much less and seems no as relevant as they used to (around 20% drop which is massive)There are more statistics to this analysis. You can check in my full article.

item.title: How to Create a PyPI Package Using Cookiecutter PyPackage
item.author: TechWithTy
item.content:encoded: Publishing your Python package to has never been easier! With , you can generate a fully structured Python package with automated testing, documentation, and CI/CD integration in just a few steps. In this guide, we’ll cover: ✅ Generating a Python package structuretesting, documentation, and PyPI deployment ✅ Releasing your package 🚀 🔹 Step 1: Install CookiecutterIf you haven’t installed , do so with:🔹 Step 2: Generate Your Python PackageRun the following command to generate a new Python package using :cookiecutter https://github.com/audreyfeldroy/cookiecutter-pypackage.git You’ll be prompted to enter details for your package, such as: (e.g., ) After completing the prompts, Cookiecutter will create a fully structured Python package.🔹 Step 3: Set Up Version ControlInitialize a Git repository and push the code to GitHub:git init git add git commit git branch main git remote add origin https://github.com/yourusername/my_pypi_package.git git push origin main 🔹 Step 4: Set Up Your Development EnvironmentTo install development dependencies, run:pip requirements_dev.txt 🔹 Step 5: Test Your PackageRun the tests using or :python unittest discover 🔹 Step 6: Register Your Package on PyPICreate an account on . in PyPI under Account Settings → API Tokens. Save your API token securely.🔹 Step 7: Configure PyPI Deployment with Travis CIIf you want , run this command to encrypt your PyPI password in Travis CI:travis encrypt deploy.password Then, add to your GitHub repo.🔹 Step 8: Publish Your Package to PyPIThen, build and upload your package:python setup.py sdist bdist_wheel twine upload dist/Enter your PyPI username and password when prompted.Automatic Upload (via GitHub Actions or Travis)Push a , and the package will be published automatically:git tag v0.1.0 git push origin v0.1.0 🔹 Step 9: Install Your PackageOnce uploaded to PyPI, your package can be installed with:pip my_pypi_package 🎯 Additional Features in Cookiecutter PyPackage✅ with or ✅ for automated testing & deployment ✅ for multi-version compatibility testing ✅ for generating API docs ✅ for automatic versioning ✅ Command-Line Interface (CLI) support via Click (optional) With , you can set up a production-ready Python package in minutes! 🚀 Want for automatic deployment? Need for linting with ? Looking for instead of ?

item.title: Announcing Griptape Framework 1.5
item.author: Kirk Crenshaw
item.content:encoded:
We’re pleased to announce that Griptape Framework 1.5 is now available. The 1.5 release brings enhancements to embeddings to add support for generating image embeddings and the addition of image search via the framework’s vector store drivers. We also have updates to the default models in several drivers, support for Perplexity with a new prompt driver and web search driver, and more. Let’s head down to the skatepark and explore some of the new features added in this release.Updated Getting Started Guide & Improved Samples First up, we have improved the Griptape Framework getting started guide on the Framework Overview documentation page. The new guide uses the python dependency manager, though of course we also provide instructions for users, and provides a tour of the key features in the framework. We recommend that you check this out, even if you're already familiar with Griptape Framework, as it covers new features added over the last few releases. We have also updated all the code samples in the documentation to include a tab showing the output logs from running the sample, so you can see the results from each sample without having to run them yourself.Support for Image Embeddings Griptape Framework 1.5 includes support for generating embeddings from objects, together with new embedding drivers for Amazon Bedrock with Amazon’s Titan Multimodal Embeddings G1 model, and for Voyage AI’s model. In the example below, I generate embeddings for 4 images that I generated using FLUX.2 with the Black Forest Labs extension for Griptape. After generating and saving two snowboarding images and two skateboarding images, in the code sample I use Amazon’s amazon.titan-embed-image-v1 model to calculate embeddings for these images and a simple relatedness calculation to compare the vectors that the embedding model generated.from griptape.drivers.embedding.amazon_bedrock import AmazonBedrockTitanEmbeddingDriver from griptape.loaders import ImageLoader import numpy as np
def calc_relatedness( x, y ): # using the same relatedness function as the localrerankdriver return np.dot(x, y) / (np.linalg.norm(x) * np.linalg.norm(y))
# create a driver for multi-modal embedding with Amazon Bedrock and Amazon Titan multi_modal_embedding_driver = AmazonBedrockTitanEmbeddingDriver( model="amazon.titan-embed-image-v1" )
# calculate embeddings for our four sample images blue_snowboarder_embeddings = multi_modal_embedding_driver.embed( ImageLoader().load("images/blue_snowboarder.jpeg") ) orange_snowboarder_embeddings = multi_modal_embedding_driver.embed( ImageLoader().load("images/orange_snowboarder.jpeg") ) beach_skater_embeddings = multi_modal_embedding_driver.embed( ImageLoader().load("images/beach_skater.jpeg") ) paris_skater_embeddings = multi_modal_embedding_driver.embed( ImageLoader().load("images/paris_skater.jpeg") )
print( # compare the two snowboarding images "blue_snowboarder vs orange_snowboarder: ", calc_relatedness(blue_snowboarder_embeddings, orange_snowboarder_embeddings), )
print( # compare a snowboarding image with a skateboarding image "blue_snowboarder vs beach_skater: ", calc_relatedness(blue_snowboarder_embeddings, beach_skater_embeddings), )
print( # compare the two skateboarding images "beach_skater vs paris_skater: ", calc_relatedness(beach_skater_embeddings, paris_skater_embeddings), ) The results are that the two snowboarding images are very related with a score of greater than 0.9 (scores closer to 1 indicate higher levels of relatedness), while the comparison of the snowboarding and skateboarding images gives a score just over 0.55. Comparing the two skateboarding images generates a relatedness score of greater than 0.75, despite one being on Santa Monica beach and the other in the skatepark at the Paris Olympics.Here are the images, together with the results for you to take a look at. I really like the results that I got with Black Forest Labs, and the results from the relatedness calculations.In addition to the changes to embedding drivers, vector store drivers have been updated to support upserting and querying with objects, and the framework’s local vector store has been updated to support persisting multi-modal entries. These changes mean that you can store the embeddings generated for the images into a vector store and then you can query this to find the top x images nearest to the embeddings for a query image. These changes enable Griptape Framework to support image-based use cases such as image similarity search. In this release we’ve added support for the popular AI-powered search and research engine, Perplexity, with the addition of and PerplexityWebSearchDriver.import os
from griptape.drivers.prompt.perplexity import PerplexityPromptDriver from griptape.rules import Rule from griptape.structures import Agent from griptape.tasks import PromptTask
agent = Agent( tasks=[ PromptTask( prompt_driver=PerplexityPromptDriver( model="sonar-pro", api_key=os.environ["PERPLEXITY_API_KEY"] ), rules=[ Rule("Be precise and concise"), ], ) ], )
agent.run("tell me about the griptape framework 1.4 release")
print(agent.output.value) If you experiment with the using the code sample above, you will notice that the responses are generated using search as well as an LLM, meaning that you get up-to-date answers from the web. In this case, the model does a great job finding some of the highlights from the last release of the framework, using the blog post about that release as a source. This makes the a little different to the other prompt drivers in Griptape Framework. We are excited to see what you build with this exciting new capability. Updates to Default Models The default model in the has been updated to .If you're using Google models, the default model for the has been updated to and the default model for the has been updated to .For developers using Amazon Bedrock to access Anthropic’s model, the default model in the AmazonBedrockPromptDriver has been updated to anthropic.claude-3-7-sonnet-20250219-v1:0. In addition, the Amazon Titan model used for text embeddings has been updated to amazon.titan-embed-text-v2:0 and the default Amazon Titan model used in the AmazonBedrockImageGenerationDriver has been updated to amazon.titan-image-generator-v2:0.As usual, you can continue to use previous generation models by setting the model keyword argument to the model that you wish to use when creating an instance of each driver.Improved Control over Tool Use If you’ve having trouble getting less powerful LLMs to function correctly when calling tools, the 1.5 release allows you to set to and have the LLM return tool outputs directly. If you're using a less powerful LLM, you should consider using this setting so that you can make tool calls yourself rather than having the LLM coordinate them. In the very simple code sample below I run three tasks where I provide a DateTimeTool. The code is commented to provide details on the behavior that you should expect, and you can see the results from running this sample below the sample.from griptape.tasks import PromptTask from griptape.tools import DateTimeTool from griptape.artifacts.list_artifact import ListArtifact
# When disabling `reflect_on_tool_use`, Task results will be returned as a ListArtifact. # Each item in the ListArtifact will be the result of a single tool execution.
date_task = PromptTask( tools=[DateTimeTool()], reflect_on_tool_use=False, ) results = date_task.run("How many days until it's 2026?") # This will fail as the model will not reflect and figure out that an additional tool run is needed to calculate the date delta
if isinstance(results, ListArtifact): for result in results: print("Simple prompt output without reflection:", result)
date_task = PromptTask( tools=[DateTimeTool()], reflect_on_tool_use=False, ) results = date_task.run("How many days from 12:44 on March 19th 2025 to Jan 1st 2026?") # This will succeed as the prompt is more specific and the model can calculate the date delta using a single tool invocation # Note that the output is the raw output from the tool
if isinstance(results, ListArtifact): for result in results: print("Detailed prompt output without reflection:", result)
date_task = PromptTask( tools=[DateTimeTool()], reflect_on_tool_use=True, ) results = date_task.run("How many days until it's 2026?") # This will succeed as the model will invoke the tool multiple times to get the current date & calculate the date delta # Note that the output is more descriptive as the model has reflected on the final tool inovcation to generate the response
print("Simple prompt output with reflection:", results) Simple prompt output without reflection: 2025-03-19 12:47:10.882934
Detailed prompt output without reflection: 287 days, 11:16:00
Simple prompt output with reflection: There are 287 days until it's 2026. ‍ Griptape Framework 1.5 is available now and you can download it with uv, poetry, pip or another Python package manager of your choice. As usual, we would love to hear your feedback on these changes, together with ideas and suggestions for future imrpovements to the framework. If you want to ask any questions about the other features in this release or discuss your image embedding use-cases, please head over to the Griptape Discord.

item.title: 🚨 The Python Debugging Playbook: Fix Your Code
item.author: 0x3d Site
item.content:encoded:
Let’s Be Real: Debugging Sucks (But It Doesn’t Have To)You’ve been there. You run your Python script, expecting it to work like magic, but instead… 🚨 Error: Something is terribly wrong. 🚨 Your stomach sinks. You stare at the error message, hoping it will just . The worst part? You have Debugging is one of the hardest (and most underrated) skills in Python. Most developers spend hours banging their heads against the wall because they never learned how to debug properly.🔹 Good debugging is not a talent—it’s a skill.Anyone can get better at it.Once you master it, you’ll code 10x faster.So, let’s fix this. In this article, I’ll walk you through a step-by-step debugging system that will make you a problem-solving beast. Step 1: Read Error Messages Like a DetectiveMost people when they see an error. Python’s error messages are —they’re They tell you: ✅ ZeroDivisionError: division by zero Instead of freaking out, break it down: → The type of error → The exact problem → Where it happened Lesson? Read every word of the error before touching your code.📌 Pro tip: If an error doesn’t make sense, search —chances are, someone has already solved it. Step 2: Print Debugging Is Overrated (Use This Instead)Everyone’s first instinct when debugging?Sure, it works. But it’s There’s a better way: Use (Python’s Built-in Debugger)When you run this, Python and lets you inspect variables . Type → Check if it's correct Type → Continue execution 🚀 This is faster than printing random values all over your code.Step 3: Isolate the Problem (Binary Search Your Code)If your code is how do you know where the bug is? 1️⃣ Comment out half your code and run it.If the error is gone, the bug was in that half.If the error remains, check the other half. until you find the exact problem. 🔹 This trick cuts debugging time in half.If you’re unsure where the bug is, test :Step 4: Google Smarter (And Stop Wasting Time)Most devs Google errors the wrong way.Bad search: (too broad)"Why does Python hate me" (not helpful) Good search:"TypeError: 'int' object is not iterable in Python""Python list append not working"🔹 The more your search, the faster you’ll find the answer.Step 5: Ask for Help (The Right Way)Asking for help isn’t a weakness—it’s a But most people ask terribly.❌ "My code doesn’t work. Help?""Getting an error. Fix it for me?"✅ Instead, use this format: Trying to sort a list but getting a TypeError. TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'str' and 'int'Removed → Works fine Used → Still fails Checked StackOverflow → No luck 🔹 This and gets you Final Thoughts: Debug Like a Pro, Code With ConfidenceThe difference between Python developers and ones? 🚀 Great devs don’t panic—they debug efficiently.Here’s your new ✅ before touching codeUse instead of debugging ✅ **Isolate the bug with a binary search approachGoogle the right way (be specific!)Ask for help properly (clear, structured, and respectful)If you follow this, you’ll and start fixing problems like a pro.
🎁 Download Free Giveaway Products We love sharing valuable resources with the community! Grab these free cheat sheets and level up your skills today. No strings attached — just pure knowledge! 🚀We've 15+ Products for FREE, just get it. We'll promise that you'll learn something out of each.Making extra income by selling websites has never been easier—AI does most of the work for you! No need to spend hours researching or figuring things out on your own. This step-by-step blueprint gives you everything you need: ✔️ A complete guide that walks you through the process ✔️ Detailed checklists so you don’t miss a thing ✔️ Pre-made ChatGPT prompts to make website creation effortless It’s all laid out for you—just follow the steps and start earning! 🚀

item.title: Why Python is the Best Programming Language for Beginners
item.author: MD SAIDUL ISLAM
item.content:encoded: 🐍 Why Python is the Best Programming Language for BeginnersAre you just starting your programming journey and wondering which language to learn first? 🤔The answer is simple: !Python is one of the most beginner-friendly, powerful, and in-demand programming languages in the world today. In this blog, we’ll explore why Python is the best choice for beginners and how you can get started.🎯 Why Should Beginners Learn Python?Python’s syntax is very similar to English, making it easy to understand even if you’ve never coded before.🔹 Compare Python with C++:#include <iostream> using namespace std; print main() { cout << "Hello,World!"; return 0;} 🔥 See how Python is simpler and cleaner?2️⃣Used Everywhere! (High Demand)Python is used in AI, Machine Learning, Web Development, Cybersecurity, Data Science, and more. Many top companies, including Google, Netflix, and NASA, use Python. 💡 : Instagram’s backend is written in Python’s Django framework!3️⃣ Huge Community & Learning ResourcesSince Python is so popular, you’ll never get stuck! There are millions of tutorials, forums, and free courses available online.4️⃣ Beginner-Friendly Yet PowerfulPython is yet powerful enough for experts. That’s why it’s used for:✅ (Django, Flask) ✅ (Pandas, TensorFlow) ✅ ✅ (Pygame)🔥 You can start with simple projects and move on to advanced topics as you grow!📌How to Start Learning Python (Step-by-Step Guide)2️⃣ Write Your First Python ProgramOnce installed, open your terminal or Python shell and type:Congrats! 🎉 You just wrote your first Python program!3️⃣ Start with Basics (Data Types, Loops, Functions, OOP)Focus on: ✅ Variables & Data Types ✅ Loops (for, while) ✅ Functions & Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)To learn faster, start building: ✅ To-Do List App ✅ Web ScraperPython is the for beginners because of its simplicity, versatility, and high demand in the job market. Start learning today, and in a few months, you’ll be building projects!🔥 Do you want a step-by-step guide on Python projects? Comment below! 🚀

item.title: A Beginner’s Guide to NumPy for Data Analysis
item.author: Meftahul Jannat Mila
item.content:encoded:
In this article, we’ll dive into NumPy, a must-know Python library that makes handling numbers and data simple and exciting. Whether you’re just starting with Python or curious about data analysis, we’ve got you covered with a friendly, step-by-step journey. We’ll explore how to work with arrays, perform calculations effortlessly, and use NumPy’s powerful tools to analyze data. To top it off, we’ll finish with a hands-on mini-project to bring everything together. Let’s embark on this adventure and unlock the magic of NumPy!Before we begin exploring NumPy, we’ll need to set up our environment to run the code examples and the mini-project later on. Here’s how we’ll get everything ready:: If Python isn’t on your system yet, we can download it from python.org. During installation, we’ll ensure the option to add Python to our PATH is checked—this makes it easier to use from the terminal.: We’ll open a terminal (or Command Prompt on Windows) and run: This tells Python’s package manager (pip) to fetch and install NumPy for us.: We’ll pick a tool to write our code. Options include:
: It comes with Python—just search for it after installation.Or any text editor we prefer!: To confirm everything works, we’ll create a file (e.g., ) in our editor and add: When we run it, seeing a version number (like ) means we’re all set!With our environment ready, we’re good to dive into NumPy!NumPy is a Python library built for numerical computations. It gives us a special data structure called an (n-dimensional array), which is faster and more efficient than regular Python lists. It’s a cornerstone of data analysis in Python and pairs wonderfully with libraries like Pandas and Matplotlib.To start using NumPy in our code, we’ll import it with:Before we go further, let’s understand why NumPy is so valuable:: It’s incredibly fast for calculations, making our work efficient.: We won’t need complex loops—NumPy handles the heavy lifting for us.: It offers a wealth of built-in functions to simplify data analysis.With these benefits in mind, let’s see what NumPy can do!Arrays are the foundation of NumPy, and we’ll explore several ways to make them.We can turn a regular Python list into a NumPy array to start working with it. transforms our list into a NumPy array.Output: — a 1D array, like a single row of numbers.We can also build a 2D array, which looks like a grid or matrix, using a list of lists.Each inner list becomes a row in our 2D array.This gives us a 2x2 matrix.NumPy lets us quickly generate arrays with specific patterns, like all zeros, ones, or a sequence.: Gives us a 2x3 array filled with .
Output: : Creates a 3x2 array of .
Output: [[1. 1.] [1. 1.] [1. 1.]]: Produces , similar to Python’s but as an array.For testing or simulations, we can generate arrays with random values.: Creates a 2x2 array of random numbers between 0 and 1.Output: Something like [[0.45 0.12] [0.78 0.33]] (values will differ each time).Understanding our array’s structure is key for analysis, so let’s look at some useful properties.: tells us we have 2 rows and 3 columns.: is the total number of elements (2 * 3).: indicates our elements are integers.NumPy simplifies math with , meaning we can skip loops entirely!We can apply operations to every element in an array with ease.: Adds 2 to each element: .: Multiplies each element: .
Array-to-Array Operations We can also combine two arrays element by element.: Performs element-wise addition: .: Performs element-wise multiplication: .For 2D arrays, we can perform matrix-specific operations like transposition or multiplication.: Flips to .: Multiplies the matrix by itself, yielding:
4. Key Functions for Data Analysis Now, let’s explore NumPy’s powerful functions that make data analysis a breeze.We can access specific parts of our arrays using indexing and slicing.: Retrieves the element at index 1.: Slices from index 1 to 2.: Fetches row 0, column 1.: selects all rows, picks column 1.NumPy offers handy tools to summarize our data statistically.: Calculates the average by summing all values and dividing by the count.: Finds the middle value when sorted.: Measures how spread out our data is./: Identifies the smallest and largest values.We can filter our data or replace values based on conditions using .: Returns indices where values exceed 3.: Extracts those values: .np.where(condition, x, y): Uses (10) where true, otherwise keeps (original value).Sometimes, we need to change an array’s shape to fit our analysis, and helps us do that.: Transforms 6 elements into a 2x3 array:
Output: .We can organize our data in order using .: Arranges the array from smallest to largest.To find distinct values in our data, we use .: Removes duplicates and sorts the result.We can summarize our data, like summing values, with aggregation functions.: Adds all elements together.: Sums down each column.: Sums across each row.
5. Mini-Project: Analyzing Random Data Now, let’s bring everything together with a fun mini-project!We’ll generate a 3x3 array of random integers, find the maximum value in each row, replace values greater than 5 with 0, and calculate the average of the resulting array.Since we’ve already set up our environment earlier, we just need to prepare a file for this project:: In our chosen editor, we’ll make a new file called .: We’ll copy the code below into this file and run it.
Example Run and Breakdown Suppose our random array looks like this:Original array: [[ 3 7 2] [ 9 4 6] [ 1 8 5]] : np.random.randint(1, 11, size=(3, 3)) generates a 3x3 array with numbers from 1 to 10.: finds the max in each row: .
means we’re looking across rows.: np.where(data > 5, 0, data) replaces 7, 9, 6, 8 with 0: [[3 0 2] [0 4 0] [1 0 5]] : computes the average: (3+0+2+0+4+0+1+0+5)/9 = 1.67.Since the numbers are random, our output will differ, but the process remains the same!Congratulations—we’ve just taken our first big step into the world of NumPy together! We’ve explored how to work with arrays, perform quick calculations, and analyze data with ease. The mini-project gave us a chance to apply these skills in a practical way, and now we’re equipped to dig deeper. NumPy opens the door to data analysis, and with a bit more practice, we can handle larger datasets or combine it with tools like Matplotlib for visuals or Pandas for structured data. Let’s keep experimenting and enjoy the exciting journey with Python and NumPy!

item.title: How to QuickBooks Workforce Employee Login Easily!
item.author: goxely
item.content:encoded:
Unlock the full potential of QuickBooks Workforce by setting up your account properly. Once configured, you gain seamless access to pay stubs, W-2 forms, and other crucial payroll documents at your convenience.Navigate with Ease: Choose Your TaskAccess Workforce and Sign InConfigure and Utilize Workforce EfficientlyCommon Inquiries AnsweredGetting Started: A Guided IntroductionWatch our instructional video for a hands-on walkthrough on configuring and leveraging QuickBooks Workforce. Keep in mind, the ability to add personal, tax, and banking details varies depending on your employer’s payroll system.Streamline Employee Management with QuickBooks Payroll Gain instant insights into employee information and simplify workforce oversight using QuickBooks Payroll’s intuitive features.Explore More Tutorials—Head to our video library for additional step-by-step guides.
Effortless Sign-In to QuickBooks Workforce Step 1: Locate Your Employer’s InvitationCheck your email inbox for a message from do_not_reply@intuit.com with your employer’s name in the subject line. If it’s missing: Look in your spam folder Notify your employer—they may have entered an incorrect emailStep 2: Accept Your InvitationOpen the email and click ‘Accept Invite’ to begin the setup.If you don’t have an Intuit account, create one using the same email provided by your employer.Already using QuickBooks Workforce or TurboTax? Simply log in with that existing Intuit credentials.
Troubleshooting Sign-In Challenges Experiencing login roadblocks? Here’s how to overcome common issues:If you already possess a QuickBooks Workforce account, you can still accept invitations from new employers. Ensure they send the invite to the email associated with your existing Workforce account.Using QuickBooks Online? You’ll need a separate email for Workforce access. We suggest reserving your work email for QuickBooks Online and using a personal email for Workforce.If your employer resends the invite, only accept the latest email to prevent login conflicts.Leveraging another Intuit product (e.g., TurboTax)? If you previously had a Workforce account, sign in using that email. If your employer used a different email, ensure you log in with the one they registered.Security Tip: Learn more about multi-factor authentication for added account protection.
Optimizing Your QuickBooks Workforce Experience Activate Pay Stub Notifications Stay informed about your earnings! Get an email alert every time a new pay stub is available.Navigate to workforce.intuit.comSelect Email NotificationEnable “Send me an email when new pay stubs are available”Access and Modify Your Information AnytimeView & download pay stubs, year-to-date earnings, and time-off details Retrieve and print W-2 forms effortlessly Update personal details, if your employer’s payroll service allows it Still stuck? QuickBooks customer support is just a call away at (855) 389-9729. They can guide you through the next steps.QuickBooks Workforce Employee login is essential for businesses that require real-time collaboration on financial data. However, connectivity issues, permission errors, or file corruption can cause disruptions. This guide outlines troubleshooting steps to resolve common multi-user mode issues. If problems persist, contact QuickBooks Customer Service at 855-389-9729 for expert assistance.
